name: Erlanger, Sidney Charles
description: Sleek, well-fed, and prosperous looking. Seems to have his chest padded with five dollar bills. Exhales an aroma of money-bags. Greatest amusement is to drink lemonade in bar-rooms, seeming thereby to form an eternal bond of friendship with the booze-clerks. Wears a capacious Al Herford overcoat in loud checks. Is extremely proud of the same. Has nice, curly hair and large feet. Is well worth your consideration.

name: Evans, Henry Simpson
description: Excellent for card-parties. Talks affably to the ladies and has cute little tricks. If you are playing bridge for keeps, take him as your partner. An experienced gambler. Runs a shell game during the summer in the country districts of western Pennsylvania. Closely identified with the Dead Beat Trust. Likes to ogle the cash-girls at Stewart's. Keen with the ladies.

name: Foster, George A.
description: To be recommended for stag-parties. Good booze companion. Very fond of beer. Also very fond of other drinks. Has religious scruples, but these do not interfere with his cheerfulness and good humor. Ambition is to be known by name to all the waiters at the Belvedere. Also has a serious side to his nature. Likes responsibility. Has a busy bustling walk, whatever the end in view. N. B.—Avoid the Johnstown flood as a topic of conversation.

name: Fuerbringer, Ralph Ottomar
clubs: Chairman of the Board of Governors of the Hullabaloo Club, and Treasurer
Adviser and Secretary Junior Debating Team
2nd Vice-President Johns Hopkins Y. M. C. A.
description: Long, lanky, and ill-nourished. Sleek and insinuating manners. Likes to talk at all times, particularly after dinner, when he performs with great satisfaction to himself and his audience. Humor of a very subtle variety, which expresses itself in stealing posters and in not coming up to various little agreements which he considers mere jokes. Known as the Scribe of Saginaw on account of his fine handwriting. N. B.—A good dinner shows on him.

name: Alfred L. Geiger
description: May be used to write up fictitious accounts of your entertainments. According to his usual newsy style, he will report some things that did happen and a great many that did not. His long experience in newspaper work qualifies him to fabricate in the most finished manner. Looks like a squirrel, and is always whistling like a bird.

name: Josias C. L. Hawkins
description: Full name, J. Clerk Lee. J. for Jay—country Jay; Clerk, on account of his grinding abilities; Lee, in honor of Guy C. Lee, the man with the hair. A student and a fair one, a poet and a bum one. Particularly distinguished for long-winded speeches and general awkwardness of manner, which he is seeking to overcome by attending the germans of the Highlandtown Social Club.

name: Humrichouse, Harry H.
clubs: Chairman J. H. U. Dance Committee, '04
description: A master of dance par excellence. Is especially graceful in the minuet. Intends to open, next October, a dance-hall for shop girls on Aisquith Street. Has the dignified, imposing, and somewhat pompous manner affected by the average floor-walker. Has a high intellectual forehead, unfortunately hollow. Also an accomplished debater by vote.

name: Jones, Andrew Dickson
clubs: Honorary Member of Classes '94 to 1904 inclusive
Honorable Mention in Dr. Browne's English
Member Monday and Tuesday German, Wednesday French, Thursday Spanish, Friday and Saturday Italian
Lunch Room Prize ('03-'04) for Consuming Largest Quantity of Raw Eggs
Edwin Litchfield Turnbull Prize for Harmony (in Dress)
Holder of Hopkins and State Record for Time Spent in Securing his Degree (10 yrs. flat)
description: Poses as a litterateur and a patron of men of letters. Also likes to see the vaudeville at the Maryland and to take the chorus girls to supper. Expects to receive his Ph.D. degree in the next eon. In the meantime, displays himself every Sunday on Charles Street for the benefit of the multitude. If wanted, may be found at any time in Walter's barber shop, undergoing his daily process of beautification.

name: Walter William Kohn
description: Noisy pugnacious walk. Self-reliant grin. Has high opinion of his knowledge of many subjects; fond of giving extempore lectures. Can make excellent biological drawings with the aid of Howe's Atlas. Smokes a pipe by the help of God.

name: Wilbur Leslie Lecron
description: Order as soon as possible. The supply of him is limited. Is saving up cigarette coupons to buy a coffin, as he is gradually dying of heart-failure due to winning events on the Hopkins track team. The University is merely an avocation with him. His true profession is smoking the cigarettes of the tobacco company for whom he travels. Is fond of girls who regard him as a hero.

name: Licking, Roy Stiles
clubs: Chairman and Speaker Class Debating Team, '02-'03, '03-'04
description: A vociferous rooter for Hopkins, sometimes, and for—himself, first, last, and all the time. Ran himself for various offices, but generally lost. Got himself elected chief rooter in order to print copies of the Hopkins songs with his name on the back as chief rooter. These he sold at five cents. Thinks he is eloquent. Makes the gestures first, and then has others write the speech to suit.

name: Lloyd, Henry L.
description: A drawing-room pet. The girls all like his overcoat and his money. Authority on after-theatre suppers,—example, lobster salad and ice cream. His highest recommend is his position among the self-elected elect. His pa says he is one of the "young men of the better sort at the University."

name: Henry C. E. Louis
description: Keen for the girls, to whom Henry Lloyd introduces him. Works in a bank, because he thinks it is pleasant to handle the cold cash. Strong on physical culture. Says physical culture develops the body, even if the mind remains uncultivated. Will start a gym for female physical culture June 1. Apply early.

name: T. Poole Maynard
description: Is very congenial at select little parties given at 12.57 A. M. Is very generous after baseball games. What's the use of being manager if you can't use the gate-receipts? Many years behind the plough at Mount Airy have done much to draw him out and make him long for the tall tumblers while engaged in the field of science.

name: McGlone, John
description: Would be excellent for ghost roles in private theatricals. Flits about the University like the departed spirit of a person pursued by the bills of the New Book Department. Is exceedingly difficult to locate.

name: Miller, Henry Clay
description: Nice, effusive manners. Is kind and affable to everyone, not excepting himself. Can gush on any subject that is brought up. Carries a cane when no one is looking at him. Says he likes little parties where violin-playing is appreciated—that is, if the social position of those present is perfectly assured. Gives Dr. Philip Ogden as a reference.

name: Muhly, Harry Edward
description: A devoted church-goer, even if he does pitch nickels. Told the Hullabaloo Board he would not buy a HULLABALOO, if his gambling was mentioned in the book. Has padlocks on his pockets. Boasts of his skill in bluffing the profs., but knows not, and knows not that they know that he knows not.

name: Neal, Clarence A.
description: Small but dignified presence. Handicapped by a serious purpose in life. Conversation of the ponderous variety suitable to afternoon teas given by elderly unmarried ladies with W. C. T. U. tendencies. Is said to attend a Y. M. C. A. Bible class. This horrible example of depravity has, however, not been fully proved.

name: Numsen, John Newman
description: Soldier by profession. Did signal service during the fire. Held up two news-boys and a milk-cart at the corner of Baltimore and Gay. In consideration of this noble deed was awarded a tin medal set in paste by adoring female friends. Attends the University by courtesy.

name: Opie, Reginald S.
description: Like the grace of God because he surpasseth understanding. Is not the whole HULLABALOO Board, but believes he is. Has great faith in his own infallibility, and in the catholicity of his aesthetic judgment. Acquired a false reputation as a hard worker. Not an athlete, but has plenty of endurance and excellent staying qualities of a particular kind. A forceful talker.

name: Sifford Pearre
description: Perfectly harmless. This saves the trouble and expense of a chaperon. Not handsome, but of immaculate habits—only vice is a keen appreciation of unsavory jokes. Would make an excellent husband, —if the qualities that last are desired. If used in this capacity, make him sign a contract as to his wife's pocket-money. His grip on the cash is very strong. Can be loosened up with appropriate flattery.

name: Frank Gibson Porter
clubs: Recording Secretary Y. M. C. A., '02-'03
description: Obtrusively unobtrusive. Set expression of typical Y. M. C. A. piety. Nose provided with a religious sniff. Very gentle. May be trusted not to say anything rude or rough on any occasion. Is secretly very proud of his shape.

name: Charles Henry Saylor
description: Serious middle-aged person of vast erudition. Opposed to all frivolity, especially hazing, that is, when he was a Freshman. Seen at the University only during lecture hours; then escapes at a run. Has a very fair opinion of himself (see Class History).

name: Schwatka, John Bushrod Jr.
description: An interesting talker, though neither innocuous nor soft-spoken. Apparently has had a wide experience of many things—in fact, is thought to be a ward-heeler among the dagoes of South Baltimore. Makes a respectable income from pitching nickels with the Freshmen. Smokes a vile-smelling pipe, and believes himself to be a drinking man.

name: Stollenwerck, Frank Jr.
description: Said by his companions to be a thorough ladies' man, but except for a faraway look in his eye, and his speech on "Petticoats in Alabama" at the Senior Banquet, we see no reason for such a supposition. Also said to be a strenuous class politician, but except for a certain hungriness of appearance, and an obstinate persistency about the payment of class dues, this accusation also is unfounded.

name: Thomas Shenton Strobhar
description: Fine, all-round entertainer. Has a varied repertoire culled from the Maryland, the Bridge, and the Bijou. As president of Dead-Beat Trust, is fully qualified to bum anything from a match to a motor-car. Gets a glass of beer every day so that he can eat ten cents' worth of free lunch, thereby doing the booze-palace five cents. Invents a new scheme every week for the gulling of the innocent.

name: Wallis, Leonard Griffith
description: Interesting, dreamy-eyed person. Uses scented soap and carries a pocket looking-glass. Stands at the windows of Levering Hall, and gives the Eutaw Street belles a chance to see what a real college boy is like. May be used with moderation to adorn the cozy corner of the Woman's College girl.

name: Wayson, Newton Edward Washington
description: Overburdened with a superfluity of name. This, however, does not detract from the charm and grace of his manner which he picked up with marvelous ease somewhere in the outlying districts of Baltimore County. Has a primitive mind, and always leaves the class banquets with an expression of mild astonishment.
